CLARITY.justification: The paper is well-organized (Intro → Related Work → Method → Experiments → Discussion → Conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early (Intro paragraph describing a novel visualization and phrase-extraction method). Notation is mostly defined (LSTM updates in Eqns. 1–6) but the decomposition section (Eqns. 8–12) has formatting/parenthesis issues and inconsistent subscripts (e.g. oT, product notation), which hurts readability. Figures/tables (e.g. Table 4, Table 6) and examples are informative, but several LaTeX artifacts, duplicated lines, and some repeated/awkward sentences (notably in the Appendix and surrounding references) suggest minor presentation and grammatical problems.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a new, LSTM-structure-aware importance metric based on an additive decomposition of c_T into per-word contributions augmented by intervening forget-gate products (Eq. 9 and the γi,j formulation in Sec. 3), which is a concrete technical novelty beyond generic gradient/saliency methods discussed in Related Work. It also introduces a practical phrase-extraction and rule-based classifier pipeline (Sec. 4) that leverages those scores and is empirically validated on sentiment and WikiMovies, and the authors explicitly contrast this with prior gate-activation and gradient-based analyses, showing clear differentiation. Overall this is a notable, non-trivial extension (informative to experts) rather than merely combining known techniques, but it is not a sweeping conceptual revolution, so I rate it a 4.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The core mathematical derivations are algebraically correct: h_T = o_T ⊙ tanh(c_T) yields the product decomposition in Eq.8–12, and the additive unrolling of c_T (Eq.9) is standard and correctly used to account for forget-gate attenuation. The experimental evidence (rule-based classifier approximating the LSTM, comparisons between cell-decomposition, cell-difference and gradient scores on Yelp/SST/WikiMovies) supports the main claims that the proposed importance measures are useful, though full quantitative tables and exact numbers are not reproduced here. Minor issues that do not invalidate the conclusions include the reliance on the final output gate o_T to attribute contributions to all words (an acknowledged approximation), arbitrary thresholds/heuristics in phrase candidate selection (c=1.1), and lack of reported variance/statistical tests or multiple runs for the experimental results.
